title: Anger at the Tax System (And What to Do With It)
dek: When the state demands its share, your body remembers every time you were forced to give more than you had. This isn't just about money; it's about the deep, somatic memory of extraction and the jaw-clenching reality of survival.
bodyMarkdown:
## The Somatic Weight of the State


*Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.239Z">May 28, 2026</time>.*

You are sitting at your kitchen table, the receipts spread out like a coroner's report. It is 11:42 PM on a Tuesday. The air in the room feels thin, as if the oxygen has been siphoned off by the sheer bureaucratic weight of the forms in front of you. Notice where your body goes first. Is it the masseter muscle, clamping down so hard your molars ache? Is it the psoas, tightening as if preparing to sprint from an invisible predator? The tax system is not just a financial obligation; it is a somatic event. It is an extraction that your body registers as a threat to your survival.

We are taught to view taxes as a civic duty, a mathematical equation to be solved once a year. But your body knows better. Your body remembers every time you were asked to give more than you had, every time your boundaries were breached by an authority you could not question. This is not pathology; this is biology. The anger that rises in your chest, hot and sharp, is not a moral failure. It is a messenger. It is telling you that the scales are unbalanced, that the extraction feels non-consensual, that the system is demanding a pound of flesh when you are already running on fumes.

## The Jaw Remembers

Let us talk about the jaw. The masseter is one of the strongest muscles in the human body, designed for crushing and grinding. When you look at the numbers on the screen, when you see the percentage of your labor that is being taken, your jaw clenches. This is the body's primitive response to powerlessness. You cannot bite the tax code. You cannot tear into the IRS with your teeth. So you bite down on yourself. You grind your teeth in your sleep, waking up with a headache that radiates up the side of your skull.

Bessel van der Kolk reminds us that the body keeps the score. The trauma of financial insecurity, the chronic stress of trying to survive in a system that feels rigged, is stored in the tissues. When you are forced to hand over the fruits of your labor to a system that often feels opaque and punitive, your nervous system registers it as a violation. The anger is a protective mechanism. It is the body's way of saying, *I matter. My labor matters. My survival matters.*

## The Psoas and the Urge to Flee

Move your attention down to your core. The psoas muscle, deep in your pelvis, connects your spine to your legs. It is the muscle of flight. When the tax bill arrives, and it is more than you anticipated, notice how your psoas contracts. You want to run. You want to escape the crushing weight of the obligation. But there is nowhere to run. The state is everywhere. And so the energy of flight gets trapped in the pelvis, manifesting as lower back pain, digestive issues, a deep, gnawing ache that no amount of stretching seems to relieve.

Resmaa Menakem writes about how trauma is decontextualized over time, looking like personality or culture. We have normalized the chronic stress of the tax season. We call it 'adulting.' We make jokes about it. But beneath the jokes is a profound somatic dysregulation. The anger you feel is not just about the money; it is about the lack of agency. It is about the feeling of being trapped in a system that does not see you, does not care about your individual circumstances, and demands compliance under threat of punishment.

## Herbal Allies for the Bureaucratic Slog

When the anger feels like a fire consuming you from the inside out, we turn to the plants. We do not use herbs to suppress the anger, to make it 'love and light.' We use them to support the nervous system so that the anger does not burn down the house. Skullcap (*Scutellaria lateriflora*) is a profound ally here. It is for the nervous system that is frayed, twitchy, overwhelmed by the details. It cools the heat of the overactive mind, the mind that is spinning out worst-case scenarios about audits and penalties.

You might also reach for Milky Oats (*Avena sativa*). This is not a quick fix; it is deep nourishment for a nervous system that has been depleted by chronic stress. When the tax system makes you feel brittle, Milky Oats brings back the juiciness, the resilience. It reminds the body that it is safe, even when the external circumstances feel threatening. [Herb Pharm Stress Manager (1 oz)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B005JW1F1C?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be taken in small doses throughout the day as you work through the forms.

## The Diaphragm and the Breath of Resistance

Notice your breathing. When you are looking at the tax forms, your breath likely becomes shallow, trapped in the upper chest. The diaphragm, the primary muscle of respiration, becomes rigid. This is the body bracing for impact. When we do not breathe fully, we signal to the nervous system that we are in danger. The anger becomes stagnant, turning into resentment and bitterness.

Peter Levine teaches us that trauma is not what happens to us, but what we hold inside in the absence of an empathetic witness. The tax system is the ultimate unempathetic witness. It does not care that your car broke down, that your child was sick, that you are exhausted. It only cares about the numbers. To counter this, you must become the empathetic witness to your own experience. You must breathe into the belly, softening the diaphragm, allowing the anger to move through you rather than getting stuck in the tissues.

## A Somatic Practice for Financial Fury

Do not try to meditate this away. Do not try to 'manifest' a better tax return. Sit in a chair with your feet flat on the floor. Feel the solid ground beneath you. Place one hand on your jaw and the other on your belly. Take a breath in, and as you exhale, let out a sound. A groan, a sigh, a growl. Let the sound vibrate through your jaw, releasing the tension in the masseter. Feel the belly expand into your hand. Acknowledge the anger. Say to yourself, *This system is extracting from me, and my body is angry about it. That is a sane response.*

If you need to move, stand up and shake. Shake your hands, your arms, your legs. Let the trapped energy of the psoas release. Imagine the bureaucratic red tape shattering around you as you move. You are not a machine. You are a living, breathing organism, and you have the right to feel the impact of the systems you are forced to participate in. [Gaia Herbs PRO Sleep Formula](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B004J32XIG?tag=spankyspinola-20) can be a supportive companion after this practice, helping to settle the nervous system back into a state of rest.

## The Illusion of the Individual

The tax system isolates us. It makes us feel like our financial struggles are a personal failure. But your anger is not just yours; it is collective. It is the anger of generations of people who have been squeezed by systems of power. When you feel that hot, sharp fury, remember that you are not alone. You are part of a vast web of bodies that are all feeling the same squeeze. The anger is a connective tissue.

We are taught to hide our financial realities, to feel shame if we cannot easily meet the demands of the state. But shame thrives in secrecy. When we bring the anger out of the shadows, when we acknowledge the somatic toll of the tax system, we begin to dismantle the isolation. We begin to see that the problem is not us; the problem is the system. And while we may not be able to change the tax code overnight, we can change how we hold the experience in our bodies.

## Reclaiming Your Energy

Every time you pay taxes, a piece of your energy is taken. But you do not have to let the system take your spirit. You can reclaim your energy by tending to your body. You can refuse to let the extraction define you. You can nourish yourself with good food, with rest, with the support of the plants. You can build resilience in your nervous system so that the next time the tax bill arrives, you are not thrown into a state of survival.

Consider incorporating a daily practice of grounding. Walk barefoot on the earth. Feel the support of the ground beneath you. The state may demand your money, but it cannot take your connection to the earth. It cannot take your breath. It cannot take your capacity to feel, to heal, to connect with others. [FES Yarrow Environmental Solution Spray (2-pack)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B00F3SKA7M?tag=spankyspinola-20) is another powerful ally here, helping the body adapt to stress and build deep reserves of energy.

## The Final Reckoning

The anger at the tax system is valid. It is a biological response to extraction and powerlessness. Do not pathologize it. Do not try to spiritualize it away. Feel it. Let it move through your muscles, your fascia, your bones. Let it inform your boundaries. Let it remind you of your own worth, a worth that cannot be quantified by a tax bracket or a deduction. You are more than your economic output. Your body knows this. Listen to it.

## The Fascia and the Hidden Tension

Let us go deeper, beneath the muscles, into the fascia. The fascial network is the connective tissue that wraps every muscle, every organ, every nerve in your body. It is a continuous web, and it is highly innervated. When you experience the chronic, low-grade stress of financial obligation, the fascia begins to dehydrate and tighten. It becomes sticky. The anger at the tax system doesn't just live in the big muscles like the psoas or the masseter; it weaves itself into the very fabric that holds you together.

You might feel this as a generalized stiffness, a feeling that your body is a size too small. You wake up in the morning and it takes ten minutes just to feel like you can stand up straight. This is the fascial response to the ongoing demand of the state. The system requires you to be rigid, to fit into its boxes and categories, and your fascia mirrors that rigidity. It is a somatic adaptation to a bureaucratic environment. To release this, we must introduce fluidity. We must move in ways that are non-linear, unpredictable, and entirely our own. We must hydrate, not just with water, but with movement that reminds the fascia of its inherent elasticity.

## The Liver and the Heat of Frustration

In Traditional Chinese Medicine, anger is associated with the Liver. The Liver is responsible for the smooth flow of Qi, or energy, throughout the body. When we are thwarted, when our agency is restricted by rules and regulations we cannot control, the Liver Qi stagnates. It builds up, creating heat. This is the heat you feel rising in your neck when you are put on hold by the IRS for the third time. It is the heat that makes your eyes feel dry and your temper short.

We do not want to suppress this Liver heat, but we also do not want it to scorch the earth. We need to move it. Bitter herbs are the traditional remedy for stagnant Liver Qi. Dandelion root, burdock, artichoke leaf - these bitter plants stimulate the liver, encouraging it to process and release the built-up tension. They remind the body how to digest the indigestible. When the tax system feels like a bitter pill to swallow, we use the literal bitterness of the plants to help the body process the experience. We support the Liver so that the anger can flow, rather than getting stuck and turning into a chronic, simmering resentment.

## The Shoulders and the Burden of Compliance

Bring your awareness to your trapezius muscles, the broad, flat muscles that span your upper back and neck. Notice how close your shoulders are to your ears right now. The tax system places a literal burden on our shoulders. It is the burden of compliance, the weight of making sure every number is correct, every box is checked, every deadline is met. The fear of making a mistake, of being penalized, causes the shoulders to hike up in a perpetual state of guarding.

This guarding is exhausting. It drains the energy that you could be using for your life, your creativity, your relationships. The levator scapulae, the muscle that lifts the shoulder blade, becomes chronically shortened. You carry the IRS on your back. To release this, you must consciously choose to put the burden down, even if just for five minutes. You must let the shoulders drop. You must remind the body that right here, right now, in this exact moment, you are safe. The forms can wait. The numbers can wait. Your somatic integrity must come first.

## FAQ

**Q: Is it normal to feel physically sick when doing taxes?**
**A:** Absolutely. The body registers the tax process as a threat to survival. The nausea, the headaches, the tight chest - these are all signs of a nervous system in fight-or-flight. It is a sane biological response to an overwhelming bureaucratic demand.

**Q: How can I stop clenching my jaw when I think about my finances?**
**A:** You cannot force the jaw to relax. You must first acknowledge the anger that is causing the clenching. Try the somatic practice of placing a hand on your jaw and making a sound. Support your nervous system with herbs like Skullcap, and give your body permission to feel the fury.

**Q: I feel guilty for being angry about taxes because I know they fund public services. What should I do?**
**A:** Two things can be true at once. You can value public services and still feel the somatic violation of the extraction. The body does not understand abstract concepts like 'civic duty'; it only understands the immediate reality of resources being taken. Honor the body's response without judging it.

**Q: Can herbs really help with financial stress?**
**A:** Herbs do not pay your taxes, but they support the nervous system that has to endure the process. Nervines like Milky Oats and Skullcap help to rebuild the frayed edges of your resilience, allowing you to navigate the stress without burning out.

---

Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality.
Last updated <time datetime="2026-05-28T00:13:18.239Z">May 28, 2026</time>.
In our work on anger at the tax system (and what to do with it), we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
</section>
